title: GPDE Workshop - Conformal invariants and nonlinear Yamabe problem on manifolds with boundary
speaker: Sophie Chen

proof_stages.label: Hessian equations and ellipticity
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from a scalar potential u, formulates Laplace, Monge-Ampere, sigma_k, and special Lagrangian equations as symmetric functions of the Hessian eigenvalues, and interprets ellipticity as monotonicity of the defining spectral function.

proof_stages.label: Lagrangian graph interpretation
proof_stages.summary: An irrotational graph (x,Du) in C^n is identified as Lagrangian, the special condition is explained as minimality/volume minimizing via calibration, and the induced metric g=I+(D^2u)^2 is introduced.

proof_stages.label: Phase geometry and divergence-form volume
proof_stages.summary: The volume element of the gradient graph is rewritten using the constant phase as an algebraic expression in elementary symmetric functions, yielding a divergence structure and identifying the critical phase where the spectral level sets become convex.

proof_stages.label: Rigidity, estimates, sharpness, and consequences
proof_stages.summary: The main qualitative and quantitative results are stated: Bernstein-type rigidity, Hessian and gradient estimates in supercritical or convex regimes, regularity of viscosity/weak solutions, and the sharpness or remaining open problems at critical and subcritical phases.

proof_stages.label: Geometric reduction of the estimate
proof_stages.summary: The desired Hessian bound is translated geometrically into controlling the slope of the special Lagrangian graph from its height, with a proof chain using mean value, Sobolev, Jacobi subharmonicity, and the divergence-form volume bound.

proof_stages.label: Volume bound from divergence structure
proof_stages.summary: The algebraic divergence formula for the volume element is used with the fundamental theorem of calculus to bound graph volume linearly by the height/potential data.

proof_stages.label: Jacobi inequality for a slope quantity
proof_stages.summary: A carefully chosen logarithmic slope quantity b, built from the Hessian eigenvalues, is shown to satisfy a strong subharmonic/Jacobi inequality with respect to the induced metric, providing control of its gradient by volume.

proof_stages.label: Sobolev, relative isoperimetry, and mean value closure
proof_stages.summary: Minimal-surface Sobolev inequalities, including a non-compact-support form equivalent to a relative isoperimetric inequality, are combined with the Michael-Simon mean value inequality to convert the integral slope control into the pointwise Hessian estimate.

examples.name: Nonquadratic harmonic zero-phase solution
examples.note: The function u=sin(x)e^y is exhibited as a nonquadratic solution of the two-dimensional zero-phase arctangent equation, showing why a lower Hessian bound or supercritical phase hypothesis is needed.
examples.engagement_reason: He gives the harmonic function sin(x)e^y as a nonquadratic zero-phase solution and says what it shows, but does not compute or verify it on the transcript.

passes.reason: the sine-exponential harmonic solution lies at the zero/critical phase in dimension two and is used to show quadratic rigidity fails without the needed phase or lower-bound hypothesis
The smooth nonquadratic harmonic function lies at the critical zero phase and is used to show the Bernstein conclusion fails without the lower-bound/strict-phase hypothesis.
the nonquadratic harmonic function solves the zero-phase special Lagrangian equation at the critical phase and is used to show Bernstein-type rigidity needs extra hypotheses.

The phase-zero harmonic solution is the critical two-dimensional special-Lagrangian boundary case and is used to show nonquadratic entire solutions can occur without the needed lower-bound hypothesis.
It is an entire nonquadratic harmonic solution at the critical zero phase, used to show quadratic rigidity fails without the lower-bound/supercritical-phase hypothesis.
The harmonic zero-phase two-dimensional special Lagrangian equation collapses to the linear Laplace case at the critical phase and is used to show the Bernstein conclusion can fail.
The entire harmonic function such as e^y sin x solves the zero critical-phase special Lagrangian equation but is nonquadratic, refuting an unqualified Bernstein statement.
It is used to refute quadratic Bernstein rigidity at the zero/critical phase without a Hessian lower bound.
It is used to show unrestricted Bernstein rigidity fails, and the zero phase is the critical phase boundary in the 2D special-Lagrangian equation.
The sine-exponential harmonic solution is nonquadratic at the critical zero phase and is used to show the Bernstein statement needs the phase or semiconvexity hypotheses.
The harmonic function such as u=sin(x)e^y is used to refute a quadratic Bernstein conclusion at the critical zero phase.
The entire harmonic function such as e^y sin x solves the zero/critical-phase special Lagrangian equation but is nonquadratic, showing the Bernstein conclusion fails without the extra hypotheses.

examples.name: Pogorelov singular Monge-Ampere counterexample
examples.note: Pogorelov's non-C^{1,1} convex solution is recalled to explain why strict convexity-type hypotheses in high-dimensional Monge-Ampere estimates are not cosmetic.
examples.engagement_reason: He sketches Pogorelov’s counterexample, describing the contracted-variable graph, its singular segment/form, and how its regularity defeats the estimate.

passes.reason: Pogorelov’s Monge-Ampere solution is singular and non-strictly-convex along a lower-dimensional set, and is invoked to destroy the hoped-for C^{1,1} estimate
Pogorelov's weak Monge-Ampere solution is singular/non-strictly-convex, with collapsed regularity, and is used to disprove a C^{1,1} estimate without strict convexity.
Pogorelov’s singular non-strictly-convex Monge-Ampere solution has a flat/singular degeneracy and is used to rule out the desired Hessian estimate without strict convexity.
The Pogorelov Monge-Ampere solution is singular and non-strictly convex, collapsing along a segment, and is cited to rule out Hessian estimates without strict convexity.
The Pogorelov solution has a singular non-strictly-convex/flat locus and is cited to show the hoped-for Hessian estimate fails without strict convexity.
Pogorelov’s singular Monge-Ampere solution has a collapsed/non-strictly-convex singular set and is invoked to defeat C^{1,1}/Hessian estimates without strict convexity.
Pogorelov's convex Monge-Ampere solution is singular/non-C^{1,1} with a flat axis, used to obstruct Hessian estimates without strict convexity.
Pogorelov’s singular weak Monge-Ampere solution with a flat/singular set is used to show C^{1,1} estimates fail without strict convexity.
Pogorelov’s singular convex solution is used explicitly to rule out the desired C^{1,1} estimate, and its flat singular set makes it structurally degenerate.
Pogorelov's singular non-C^{1,1} solution with a flat segment lacks strict convexity and is used to defeat Monge-Ampere Hessian estimates.
Pogorelov’s singular Monge-Ampere solution is invoked to show C^{1,1} estimates fail without strict convexity and has a collapsed singular/flat set.
Pogorelov’s Monge-Ampere solution is singular and non-C^{1,1} with a flat/failed-strict-convexity locus, and is used to defeat the desired Hessian estimate without strict convexity.

examples.name: Subcritical C^{1,1/3} special Lagrangian solution
examples.note: A C^{1,1/3} subcritical-phase solution, heuristically motivated by rotating a cubic curve and then integrating a gradient graph, shows the sharp obstruction to C^{1,1} estimates below the critical phase.
examples.engagement_reason: He explains the construction geometrically via a cubic curve, coordinate rotation, gradient graph, and integration to obtain the C^{1,1/3} solution.

passes.reason: the subcritical special Lagrangian solution has only C^{1,1/3} regularity from a cusp-like gradient graph and is used to show estimates fail below the critical phase
The subcritical special-Lagrangian solution has only C^{1,1/3} cusp-type regularity and is used to show regularity/estimates fail below the critical phase.
the subcritical C^{1,1/3} special Lagrangian solution is a singular low-regularity weak solution used to show regularity estimates fail below the critical phase.
The subcritical special-Lagrangian solution has only C^{1,1/3} cusp regularity and is presented as the counterexample showing regularity/estimate failure below the critical phase.
The subcritical special-Lagrangian solution has cusp-like only C^{1,1/3} regularity and is used to refute regularity/estimate statements below the critical phase.
The subcritical C^{1,1/3} special Lagrangian solution is a low-regularity cusp-type weak solution used to show regularity estimates fail below the critical phase.
The subcritical special Lagrangian solution has a cubic-cusp C^{1,1/3} singularity and is used to show regularity estimates fail below the critical phase.
The subcritical C^{1,1/3} cusp-like special Lagrangian solution is used to show regularity estimates fail below the critical phase.
The C^{1,1/3} subcritical-phase special-Lagrangian solution is presented as an obstruction to regularity estimates and is singular/low-regularity rather than smooth.
The subcritical C^{1,1/3} cusp-type special Lagrangian solution is low-regularity and is used to refute regularity or estimates below the critical phase.
The subcritical C^{1,1/3} special Lagrangian solution is used as an irregular example obstructing regularity estimates below the critical phase.
The subcritical special Lagrangian construction has only C^{1,1/3} cusp-type regularity, and is used to show regularity or C^{1,1} estimates fail below the critical phase.
